Munich Re has issued three green bonds: one US dollar-denominated bond (US$ 1.25bn issued in 2022) and two euro-denominated bonds (€1bn and €1.25bn issued in 2021 and 2020, respectively) [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0109__munich-re-s-issued-green-bonds]. As of December 31, 2023, the volume of issued green bonds was €2,250m and US$ 1,250m [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0110__green-bonds].

Key details regarding these bonds include:
*   **Objectives:** The bonds aim to support the Paris Agreement and the strategic goal of increasing investments in renewable energies and energy efficiency to fight climate change [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0109__munich-re-s-issued-green-bonds].
*   **Allocation:** Net proceeds are allocated to eligible projects per Munich Re's Green Bond Framework [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0109__munich-re-s-issued-green-bonds]. For example, the 2022 bond's proceeds were allocated to seven projects across Australia, the United States, and Europe [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0109__munich-re-s-issued-green-bonds].
*   **Governance:** The Green Bond Committee governs the selection and monitoring of eligible projects, reviewing them annually against exclusions and eligibility criteria set out in the RIG and the Green Bond Framework [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0109__munich-re-s-issued-green-bonds][munich_re_sustainability_2023__0110__green-bonds].
*   **Reporting:** Munich Re commits to publishing an allocation and impact report on its website one year after issuance and annually thereafter until full allocation, or if material changes to allocation occur [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0110__green-bonds]. The Green Bond Allocation and Impact Report 2023 was audited with limited assurance by an external auditor [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0277__8-1-about-the-report].

Swiss Re provides the following information regarding its single-risk reinsurance offering:

*   **Definition and Use:** Single-risk reinsurance (also referred to as facultative reinsurance) is a form of reinsurance where each risk or policy is negotiated and agreed upon individually [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0007__swiss-re-s-business-model][swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0123__environment]. It is typically utilized for larger commercial assets that exceed the coverage provided by a treaty reinsurance contract [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0218__single-risk-re-insurance].
*   **Climate Alignment and Targets:** Swiss Re has climate alignment targets for 2025 and 2030 to gradually increase the share of GWP aligned to net zero by 2050 within selected single-risk P&C re/insurance portfolios [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0218__single-risk-re-insurance]. In 2024, these targets covered 6% of the total P&C business, or 23% of the total GWP of the single-risk P&C re/insurance portfolio [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0218__single-risk-re-insurance].
*   **Renewable Energy:** Swiss Re writes single-risk re/insurance for various renewable energy power plants, including solar, wind, biomass, geothermal, hydro, and marine/tidal [13 sigma 5/2022: Maintaining resilience as a new world order takes shape, Swiss Re.][swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0157__advancing-the-net-zero-transition]. In 2024, it wrote cover for 21,573 wind and solar power plants and 329 other renewable power plants [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0138__approach-in-underwriting][swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0157__advancing-the-net-zero-transition].
*   **Underwriting Restrictions and Policies:**
    *   **Thermal Coal:** Since 2018, Swiss Re does not provide single-risk re/insurance to projects or companies with more than 30% exposure to thermal coal [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0138__approach-in-underwriting]. It also restricts single-risk business for thermal coal assets of companies planning new power capacity of at least 100MW or new mining sites; this restriction applied to new property line clients in mid-2024, extending to existing property clients in mid-2025 and other lines in mid-2026 [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0138__approach-in-underwriting].
    *   **Environmental Impacts:** For single-risk transactions, Swiss Re does not support activities that cause degradation or conversion of Ramsar wetlands, IUCN protected areas categories I-IV, or UNESCO World Heritage Sites, nor activities associated with ongoing, severe, and unmitigated pollution or waste issues [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0123__environment].
    *   **Exceptions:** Exceptions to thermal coal restrictions may apply for transactions involving coal mine reclamation (for mines operational before 2018), operational carbon capture and storage targeting all plant emissions, or transactions supporting the accelerated decommissioning of a thermal coal power plant (subject to individual evaluation of the client's climate commitment) [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0138__approach-in-underwriting]. Additionally, in low- and middle-income countries where coal provides over 70% of electricity, existing power plants operational before 2018 may be covered until 2025 if an effective emission reduction strategy is implemented [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0138__approach-in-underwriting].

PRA Supervisory Statement 5/25 applies to banks and insurers, referred to as 'firms' [pra_ss5-25_climate__0003__1-introduction].
PRA SS5/25 commenced on 3 December 2025 and replaces SS3/19 in its entirety [pra_ss5-25_climate__0008__3-implementation].

According to the PRA, the two primary transmission channels through which climate-related risks affect firms are physical risks and transition risks [pra_ss5-25_climate__0006__2-how-climate-related-risks-affect-firms].

The PRA expects insurers to use Climate Scenario Analysis (CSA) as a key tool to identify, quantify, and manage climate-related risks, particularly because historic data is insufficient for these risks [chunk_id=pra_ss5-25_climate__0027__role-of-scenario-analysis]. Specific expectations include:

*   **Proportionality and Sophistication:** The use of CSA should be proportionate to the materiality of the risks. While smaller firms may use less sophisticated approaches, larger firms with material exposure are expected to use more mathematically sophisticated methods [chunk_id=pra_ss5-25_climate__0027__role-of-scenario-analysis].
*   **Integration with ORSA:** Insurers must include CSA in their Stress and Scenario Testing (SST) component of their Own Risk and Solvency Assessment (ORSA), unless the impact is immaterial [chunk_id=pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa]. They must consider the latest climate science and advances in scenario modelling [chunk_id=pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa].
*   **Granularity and Scenarios:** Insurers should build scenarios sufficiently granular to stress specific risks they face (e.g., tropical storms, flooding, credit risk) using parameters like sea surface temperatures or GDP [chunk_id=pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa]. They should use a mix of narrative-based scenarios quantified by expert judgement and more sophisticated approaches [chunk_id=pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa][chunk_id=pra_ss5-25_climate__0027__role-of-scenario-analysis].
*   **Management Actions:** Insurers must specify management actions (e.g., changes to underwriting or investment strategy) and suitable trigger points for implementation in their ORSA [chunk_id=pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa]. They must be prudent regarding assumptions on market availability and liquidity, considering the systemic nature of climate scenarios [chunk_id=pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa].
*   **Reputational Risk:** Insurers should consider climate-related reputational risks arising from their strategies and public commitments, including the risk of 'greenwashing' claims [chunk_id=pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa].
*   **Modeling and Governance:** Insurers should use conceptually sound models supported by scientific research, acknowledge model limitations (such as non-linearities), and conduct sensitivity analysis [chunk_id=pra_ss5-25_climate__0027__role-of-scenario-analysis][chunk_id=pra_ss5-25_climate__0031__scenario-governance-controls-and-review]. The board must understand the capabilities, limitations, and inputs of the CSA tools used [chunk_id=pra_ss5-25_climate__0031__scenario-governance-controls-and-review].
*   **Use Cases:** CSA should inform business strategy, risk management, capital setting (via ORSA), and valuation [chunk_id=pra_ss5-25_climate__0028__scenario-selection-and-use-cases__pf00].

The PRA expects insurers to include the following in their Own Risk and Solvency Assessment (ORSA) regarding climate-related risks:

*   Processes to consider the impact on capital levels of reasonably foreseeable adverse scenarios, including material climate-related risks, as part of capital management plans [chunk_id=pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa].
*   If a firm accepts a material risk, the ORSA must explain why this was considered appropriate [chunk_id=pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa].
*   Climate Scenario Analysis (CSA) as part of the Stress and Scenario Testing (SST) component, unless the impact is immaterial [chunk_id=pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa].
*   Consideration of the latest climate science and advances in climate scenario modelling [chunk_id=pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa].
*   Scenarios that are sufficiently granular to stress for specific risks faced by the insurer (e.g., tropical storms, flooding, longevity risk, credit risk) and build on parameters such as sea surface temperatures, precipitation, GDP, inflation, interest rates, and unemployment rates [chunk_id=pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa].
*   Specification of management actions to be taken in different circumstances, including triggers for those actions (e.g., changes to underwriting or investment strategy) [chunk_id=pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa].
*   Consideration of climate-related reputational risks arising from investment and underwriting strategies, historical activities, and engagement on climate change [chunk_id=pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa].

According to the PRA, non-life insurers should approach underwriting risk in the context of climate change by considering the impact on natural catastrophe risk and assessing whether this impact is sufficiently factored into quantitative tools used for assessing present-day or future weather-related perils [chunk_id=pra_ss5-25_climate__0051__underwriting-and-reserving-risk]. Insurers should make material adjustments to reflect their own view of such impacts and consider how climate change might lead to larger claims than expected from historical experience alone [chunk_id=pra_ss5-25_climate__0051__underwriting-and-reserving-risk]. Additionally, during model validation, insurers should explicitly consider how climate change impacts are reflected in the modelling of climate-related perils that significantly contribute to the Solvency Capital Requirement (SCR) [chunk_id=pra_ss5-25_climate__0051__underwriting-and-reserving-risk].

For liability insurances, non-life insurers should consider the potential for climate change to lead to an accumulation of claims, allowing for the possibility of claims emerging under multiple contracts and underwriting years where applicable [chunk_id=pra_ss5-25_climate__0051__underwriting-and-reserving-risk]. Where exposures to these types of claims are large, modelling of non-natural catastrophes should include an allowance for climate claims [chunk_id=pra_ss5-25_climate__0051__underwriting-and-reserving-risk]. Furthermore, non-life insurers should ensure that their Technical Provisions (TPs) include expected losses from climate-related risks under policies already underwritten, and unearned premium reserves should allow for the impact of climate-related risk on expected weather losses, recognizing that past experience may not indicate future experience [chunk_id=pra_ss5-25_climate__0054__regulatory-balance-sheet].

Based on the provided sources, the EIOPA governance requirements for fit and proper persons are as follows:

**Fit Requirements**
National competent authorities must ensure that persons who effectively run the undertaking or hold key functions (including administrative, supervisory, or management bodies) are "fit" [chunk_id_5]. This involves:
*   Taking account of duties allocated to individual persons to ensure appropriate diversity of qualifications, knowledge, and relevant experience [chunk_id_5].
*   Ensuring that members of the administrative, management, or supervisory body collectively possess appropriate qualification, experience, and knowledge regarding:
    *   Insurance and financial markets [chunk_id_5].
    *   Business strategy and business model [chunk_id_5].
    *   System of governance [chunk_id_5].
    *   Financial and actuarial analysis [chunk_id_5].
    *   Regulatory framework and requirements [chunk_id_5].

**Proper Requirements**
When assessing whether a person is "proper," the undertaking must assess the person's honesty and financial soundness based on relevant evidence regarding their character, personal behavior, and business conduct [chunk_id_5]. This assessment includes any criminal, financial, or supervisory aspects regardless of jurisdiction [chunk_id_5]. The period of limitation for committed offences is judged based on national law or practice [chunk_id_5].

**Policy and Procedures**
National competent authorities must ensure the undertaking has a policy on fit and proper requirements that includes:
*   A description of the procedure for assessing the fitness and propriety of persons effectively running the undertaking or holding key functions, both when considered for a specific position and on an ongoing basis [chunk_id_1].
*   A description of situations that give rise to a re-assessment of fit and proper requirements [chunk_id_1].
*   A description of fit and proper procedures for assessing other relevant personnel not subject to Article 42 of the Solvency II Directive, according to internal standards, both when considered for a specific position and on an ongoing basis [chunk_id_1].

Based on the provided sources, EIOPA requires the following regarding the outsourcing of key functions to service providers:

*   **Fit and Proper Procedures:** National competent authorities (NCAs) must ensure that undertakings apply fit and proper procedures to assess persons employed by the service provider or sub-service provider performing an outsourced key function [chunk_id=eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0015__guideline-14-outsourcing-of-key-function].
*   **Designation of Responsible Person:** Undertakings must designate a person within the undertaking with overall responsibility for the outsourced key function. This person must be fit and proper and possess sufficient knowledge and experience to challenge the performance and results of the service provider [chunk_id=eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0015__guideline-14-outsourcing-of-key-function].
*   **Written Policy:** Undertakings must include their approach and processes for outsourcing in a written policy, covering the period from inception to the end of the contract. This policy must detail:
    *   Criteria for determining if a function is critical or important [chunk_id=eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0048__guideline-47-outsourcing-written-policy].
    *   How suitable service providers are selected and how/when their performance is assessed [chunk_id=eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0048__guideline-47-outsourcing-written-policy].
    *   Details to be included in the written agreement with the service provider [chunk_id=eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0048__guideline-47-outsourcing-written-policy].
    *   Business contingency plans, including exit strategies for critical or important functions [chunk_id=eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0048__guideline-47-outsourcing-written-policy].
*   **Determination of Criticality:** Undertakings must determine and document whether an outsourced function is critical or important based on whether it is essential to the undertaking's operation and the delivery of services to policyholders [chunk_id=eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0040__guideline-39-coordination-of-the-calcula].
*   **Intermediaries:** If an insurance intermediary (not an employee) is given authority to underwrite business or settle claims, this activity is subject to outsourcing requirements [chunk_id=eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0040__guideline-39-coordination-of-the-calcula].
*   **Intra-group Outsourcing:** If key functions are outsourced within a group, the responsible entity must document which functions relate to which legal entity and ensure that the performance of key functions at the undertaking level is not impaired [chunk_id=eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0040__guideline-39-coordination-of-the-calcula].

According to EIOPA Guidelines on System of Governance (Guideline 24), an undertaking's liquidity risk management policy should cover at least the following items [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0025__guideline-24-liquidity-risk-management-p]:

a) The procedure for determining the level of mismatch between the cash inflows and the cash outflows of both assets and liabilities, including expected cash flows of direct insurance and reinsurance such as claims, lapses or surrenders [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0025__guideline-24-liquidity-risk-management-p].
b) Consideration of total liquidity needs in the short and medium term, including an appropriate liquidity buffer to guard against a liquidity shortfall [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0025__guideline-24-liquidity-risk-management-p].
c) Consideration of the level and monitoring of liquid assets, including a quantification of potential costs or financial losses arising from an enforced realisation [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0025__guideline-24-liquidity-risk-management-p].
d) Identification and costs of alternative financing tools [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0025__guideline-24-liquidity-risk-management-p].
e) Consideration of the effect on the liquidity situation of expected new business [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0025__guideline-24-liquidity-risk-management-p].

Additionally, regarding reinsurance, the risk management policy should include liquidity management to deal with any timing mismatch between claims' payments and reinsurance recoverable [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0022__guideline-21-reinsurance-and-other-risk].

Under EIOPA's Guideline 18, an undertaking's underwriting and reserving risk management policy must cover at least the following items [chunk_id=eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0019__guideline-18-underwriting-and-reserving]:

a) The types and characteristics of the insurance business, such as the type of insurance risk the undertaking is willing to accept [chunk_id=eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0019__guideline-18-underwriting-and-reserving].
b) How the adequacy of premium income to cover expected claims and expenses is to be ensured [chunk_id=eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0019__guideline-18-underwriting-and-reserving].
c) The identification of the risks arising from the undertaking's insurance obligations, including embedded options and guaranteed surrender values in its products [chunk_id=eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0019__guideline-18-underwriting-and-reserving].
d) How, in the process of designing a new insurance product and the premium calculation, the undertaking takes account of the constraints related to investments [chunk_id=eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0019__guideline-18-underwriting-and-reserving].
e) How, in the process of designing a new insurance product and the premium calculation, the undertaking takes account of reinsurance or other risk mitigation techniques [chunk_id=eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0019__guideline-18-underwriting-and-reserving].

Munich Re will not accept stand-alone covers for Arctic oil and gas drilling activities, including directly related infrastructure and installations [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0056__arctic-oil-and-gas]. This restriction also applies to mixed covers and treaty reinsurance business where the exposure exceeds a defined single-digit percentage threshold [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0056__arctic-oil-and-gas]. The policy allows minor exceptions in the Norwegian region south of the Arctic Circle, as Arctic conditions do not prevail there [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0056__arctic-oil-and-gas]. Munich Re defines the Arctic region based on the internationally accepted definition by the Arctic Monitoring and Assessment Programme (AMAP) [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0056__arctic-oil-and-gas]. These underwriting restrictions apply to renewals of existing reinsurance treaty business with commencement dates of 2023 or later [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0056__arctic-oil-and-gas].

Munich Re stopped insuring these projects on 1 April 2023 [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0054__oil-and-gas]. A 'new' oil and gas field is defined as one where no prior production had taken place as of 31 December 2022 [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0054__oil-and-gas].

Munich Re has committed to transitioning its underwriting portfolio to net-zero GHG emissions by 2050 [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0153__liabilities]. The Climate Ambition 2025 serves as an important milestone on the way to achieving this commitment [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0153__liabilities].

Swiss Re's Climate Transition Plan (CTP) is the company's first such plan, published as required by the Swiss Ordinance on Climate Disclosures [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0049__climate-transition-plan]. It outlines Swiss Re's net-zero transition approach and corresponding action plan for decarbonising its business while developing related opportunities [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0049__climate-transition-plan][swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0207__climate-transition-plan].

The plan describes a transition approach centred on a decarbonisation strategy that includes three key levers: jointly transition by engaging with clients, investees and vendors; manage portfolios; and reduce operational emissions [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0049__climate-transition-plan]. It also outlines Swiss Re's carbon removal strategy and transition-enabling activities that help address critical dependencies and uncertainties [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0049__climate-transition-plan]. The action plan builds on Swiss Re's existing climate targets and mitigating actions already implemented across underwriting, investments, and operations [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0049__climate-transition-plan].

Swiss Re's Thermal Coal Policy applies the following thresholds for single-risk business [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0138__approach-in-underwriting]:

*   **Exposure Threshold:** Swiss Re does not provide re/insurance to companies or projects with more than 30% exposure to thermal coal [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0138__approach-in-underwriting].
*   **New Capacity Restriction:** The policy restricts thermal coal assets of companies planning to develop new thermal coal mining sites or power capacity of at least 100MW [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0138__approach-in-underwriting]. This restriction applies to new clients for the property line of business as of mid-2024, and to existing clients as of mid-2025 (for property) and mid-2026 (other lines of business) [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0138__approach-in-underwriting].
*   **Exceptions:** Exceptions apply for transactions in low- and middle-income countries where more than 70% of electricity is derived from coal, allowing coverage of existing power plants (operational before 2018) until 2025 if an effective emission reduction strategy is implemented [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0138__approach-in-underwriting]. Additional exceptions exist for transactions with operational carbon capture and storage targeting all emissions, coal mine reclamation for mines operational before 2018, and exceptional cases supporting accelerated decommissioning of thermal coal power plants [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0138__approach-in-underwriting].

Swiss Re does not support single-risk re/insurance transactions for the following oil and gas companies:

*   Companies that produce the world's 10% most carbon-intensive oil and gas, measured as CO2 lifecycle emissions per barrel, based on Rystad data [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0135__approach-in-underwriting].
*   Companies with an annual oil and gas production of more than 10 million barrels of oil equivalent based on Rystad data [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0135__approach-in-underwriting].
*   Companies with more than 10% of upstream production located in the Arctic Monitoring and Assessment Programme (AMAP) region (excluding Norway) [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0135__approach-in-underwriting].

Additionally, Swiss Re does not support standalone upstream projects within new oil and gas fields (i.e., those that received a final investment decision after 2022) or standalone upstream oil and gas projects located in the AMAP region (excluding Norway) [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0135__approach-in-underwriting].

Swiss Re's ESG Risk Framework comprises three umbrella guidelines and six sector-specific policies [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0124__umbrella-guidelines-and-sector-specific][swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0126__governance].

Swiss Re’s ESG risk assessment and escalation process for underwriting transactions operates as follows:

**1. ESG Risk Assessment**
The first step involves using an ESG risk assessment tool to evaluate potential transactions, typically single-risk business [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0150__esg-risk-assessment]. This tool classifies transactions as low, medium, or high risk, or provides a direct recommendation to abstain, while offering guidance on potential ESG exposures [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0150__esg-risk-assessment]. The tool utilizes a proprietary database containing a country/sector risk matrix and a watch list of companies and projects, which is updated with information from public sources and third-party providers [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0150__esg-risk-assessment]. For treaty business, assessments are conducted when data on the underlying risk is available, with specific approaches for sectors like thermal coal, defense, and oil and gas [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0150__esg-risk-assessment].

**2. ESG Risk Referral**
High-risk transactions identified by the assessment tool require a mandatory referral to internal sustainability risk managers for in-depth analysis [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0152__esg-risk-referral-and-escalation-process]. Business practitioners may also submit referrals for any potential transaction based on additional research or underwriting judgment, even if not classified as high risk [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0152__esg-risk-referral-and-escalation-process]. Sustainability risk managers analyze these referrals using data from public sources and independent third-party research, applying a 5% materiality threshold based on the re/insured's revenue or total re/insured value [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0152__esg-risk-referral-and-escalation-process].

**3. Outcomes and Conditions**
The outcome of an ESG risk referral is a binding recommendation to proceed, proceed with conditions, or abstain [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0152__esg-risk-referral-and-escalation-process]. If conditions are applied, they may require the client to provide evidence or monitor risk mitigation measures, and these transactions undergo mandatory follow-up to verify adherence [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0152__esg-risk-referral-and-escalation-process].

**4. Escalation Process**
Recommendations to abstain or proceed with conditions can be appealed by the business practitioner and escalated to the next management level [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0152__esg-risk-referral-and-escalation-process]. While both business and risk management factors are considered during escalation, the final decision-making authority remains with Risk Management and Swiss Re's Group Chief Risk Officer [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0152__esg-risk-referral-and-escalation-process].

**5. Effectiveness and Integration**
The ESG Risk Framework is integrated into underwriting guidelines, and the assessment tool is embedded in underwriting workflow tools with adherence checks for single-risk business every six months [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0153__effectiveness]. Breaches are recorded, and corrective actions are taken to prevent future occurrences [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0153__effectiveness]. All new employees must complete mandatory online training on the ESG Risk Framework, and client-facing roles receive regular refreshers [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0153__effectiveness].

An impact tolerance is defined as the maximum tolerable level of disruption to an important business service as measured by a length of time in addition to any other relevant metrics [pra_ss1-21_operational_resilience__0004__setting-an-impact-tolerance__pf00]. It is set at the point at which any further disruption to the important business service would pose a risk to the firm's safety and soundness, and in the case of insurers, policyholder protection, and, if a firm meets the criteria as set out in the Operational Resilience Parts, the financial stability of the UK [pra_ss1-21_operational_resilience__0004__setting-an-impact-tolerance__pf00]. Impact tolerances are defined as the maximum tolerable amount of disruption and should apply at peak times as well as in normal circumstances [pra_ss1-21_operational_resilience__0004__setting-an-impact-tolerance__pf00].

After 31 March 2025, the PRA expects maintaining operational resilience to be a dynamic activity [chunk_id_1]. By this date, firms should have sound, effective, and comprehensive strategies, processes, and systems that enable them to address risks to their ability to remain within their impact tolerance for each important business service in the event of a severe but plausible disruption [chunk_id_1].

Munich Re and Swiss Re differ in their fossil fuel exclusion criteria primarily regarding the specific thresholds for coal revenue, the scope of oil and gas restrictions, and the application of these rules to insurance versus investment portfolios.

**Coal Exclusion Thresholds**
*   **Swiss Re** applies a 5% revenue threshold for thermal coal mining or power generation for direct investments, with this threshold applying to corporate bond holdings with maturity beyond 2030 [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0260__swiss-re-s-fossil-fuel-related-threshold]. Additionally, Swiss Re aims to fully exit coal mining and coal-fired power generation companies via normal portfolio reallocations by 2030 [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0258__monitoring-climate-risks].
*   **Munich Re** excludes companies generating more than 30% of their revenue from thermal coal from its investment universe. Companies with revenue between 15% and 30% are also excluded or actively supported in transition [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0100__defined-exclusion-criteria].

**Oil and Gas Restrictions**
*   **Swiss Re** avoids investments in the 10% most carbon-intensive oil and gas companies and restricts direct investments in companies with more than 10% of upstream production in the AMAP region (excluding Norway) [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0258__monitoring-climate-risks][swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0260__swiss-re-s-fossil-fuel-related-threshold]. For direct infrastructure debt and corporate private placements, Swiss Re applies full restrictions on upstream oil and gas, limits midstream to pipeline/distribution/storage, and restricts downstream to specific types (e.g., peaking plants for gas) with maximum maturities of 2030 for oil and 2035 for gas [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0260__swiss-re-s-fossil-fuel-related-threshold].
*   **Munich Re** focuses its insurance exclusions on new oil and gas fields, new midstream infrastructure, and new oil-fired power plants that were not under construction or in operation as of 31 December 2022 [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0054__oil-and-gas]. For oil sands, Munich Re excludes business for new or existing sites or infrastructure where exposure exceeds a threshold of 20% of the sum insured or revenue [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0054__oil-and-gas].

**Scope of Application**
*   **Swiss Re** applies its fossil fuel guidelines to its corporate bond, listed equity, sovereign bond, real estate, direct infrastructure debt, corporate private placements, and primary private equity funds [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0258__monitoring-climate-risks][swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0260__swiss-re-s-fossil-fuel-related-threshold].
*   **Munich Re** explicitly states that its oil and gas insurance exclusions apply to its primary insurance and facultative and direct (re)insurance business [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0054__oil-and-gas]. Regarding investments, Munich Re defines exclusions for its own direct investment portfolio regardless of asset managers [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0100__defined-exclusion-criteria].

Swiss Re's decarbonization approach is centered on a strategy that includes three key levers: jointly transition by engaging with clients, investees and vendors; manage portfolios; and reduce operational emissions [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0049__climate-transition-plan]. This approach is outlined in its Climate Transition Plan (CTP) and aims to reach net-zero greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions across its entire business by 2050 [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0049__climate-transition-plan][swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0054__group-sustainability-strategy-developmen].

Munich Re's approach involves analyzing the consequences of climate change in the financial services and insurance sector, leveraging a large scientific network to access the latest findings on climate change [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0117__3-3-climate-related-disclosure]. These findings are consolidated and translated into recommendations for internal use and clients, with climate risks and opportunities integrated into Munich Re's strategy through governance, strategy, risk management, metrics, and targets following TCFD recommendations [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0117__3-3-climate-related-disclosure].

Swiss Re monitors climate-related risks by closely monitoring the climate change litigation (CCL) landscape, new regulations, and potential claims scenarios, evaluating CCL activity under five different claims scenarios [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0242__climate-change-litigation-risk]. It identifies industries most exposed to transition risks by assessing GHG emissions and managing risks through portfolio reallocations, monitoring GHG emissions of corporate bond and listed equity portfolios on an ongoing basis, and monitoring sovereign bond and parts of its real estate portfolios annually [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0258__monitoring-climate-risks]. Swiss Re also applies forward-looking indicators, such as temperature scores, to its corporate bond portfolio to assess how emissions and net-zero transition plans translate into global warming [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0258__monitoring-climate-risks]. Additionally, Swiss Re actively monitors and manages re/insurance portfolios potentially exposed to physical and transition risks, determining relevance based on quantitative thresholds (5% of total premium, reserves, or expected profits) and qualitative factors, including portfolios where weather-related perils are the dominant loss driver [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0227__underwriting].

Munich Re monitors various types of climate-related risks through specialised departments, integrating them into its risk management system in line with TCFD recommendations, differentiating between physical and transition risks [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0126__climate-change-risk]. Munich Re applies different time horizons for assessing risks and opportunities: short-term (up to 2 years), medium-term (3-10 years), and long-term (more than 10 years) [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0125__climate-change-time-horizons].

Munich Re highlights innovative insurance solutions for ESG risks, including coverage for people exposed to heavy burdens due to financial or life circumstances, and products for public sector business [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0062__aiming-for-positive-impact-through-insur]. The company aims to support Paris climate targets by insuring new, climate-friendly technologies and making them financially viable for investors [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0048__3-sustainability-in-business].

Swiss Re offers re/insurance solutions for transition-technology-related projects, including renewable energy, battery energy storage systems, and direct air capture technology [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0157__advancing-the-net-zero-transition]. Specific products include single-risk re/insurance for wind and solar power plants, as well as cover for hydropower, geothermal, marine/tidal, and biomass plants [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0157__advancing-the-net-zero-transition]. Additionally, Swiss Re provides a first-of-its-kind insurance for carbon credit forwards [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0157__advancing-the-net-zero-transition].

The PRA expects firms to regularly carry out risk assessments to identify material climate-related risks and understand how these risks affect business model resilience over relevant time horizons and under different climate scenarios [pra_ss5-25_climate__0022__risk-identification-and-assessment]. To structure this assessment, firms should:

*   Identify transmission channels through which physical and transition risks impact risk types (e.g., market, credit, liquidity, operational, underwriting, reserving, reputational, and litigation risks) and future revenue and profitability, with granularity reflecting the firm's business model and risk profile [pra_ss5-25_climate__0022__risk-identification-and-assessment].
*   Determine which combinations of transmission channels and risk types materially impact the business model, identify affected business lines, and consider the risk imminence (short or long-term) [pra_ss5-25_climate__0022__risk-identification-and-assessment].
*   Include all material climate-related risks in the firm risk register, linking each entry to an existing financial or operational risk type and clearly articulating the transmission channel [pra_ss5-25_climate__0022__risk-identification-and-assessment].
*   Exercise judgment to assess risk materiality, providing sufficient detail on methodologies, assumptions, data, proxies, and governance to allow for effective challenge [pra_ss5-25_climate__0022__risk-identification-and-assessment].
*   Consider appropriate time horizons for impact analysis, including factors such as market expectations of future events, instrument maturities, business model adaptation timescales, counterparty refinancing risks, and expected insurance liability run-off [pra_ss5-25_climate__0022__risk-identification-and-assessment].
*   Subject risk identification and assessment to periodic review to ensure materiality assessments are up-to-date based on the latest scientific evidence and that no material risks are unrecognized [pra_ss5-25_climate__0022__risk-identification-and-assessment].
*   Go beyond historical data by including forward-looking tools such as scenario analysis and stress testing as appropriate [pra_ss5-25_climate__0022__risk-identification-and-assessment].

Regarding measurement and monitoring, the PRA expects firms to have robust risk management frameworks that enable them to effectively identify, measure, monitor, manage, and report risks, with controls commensurate with the nature, scale, and complexity of their business model [pra_ss5-25_climate__0020__business-strategy]. Firms should ensure competent and independent control functions oversee these frameworks [pra_ss5-25_climate__0020__business-strategy]. The board should ensure mechanisms are in place for the periodic review of the firm's strategy, risk appetite, and risk management practices, reflecting the rapidly evolving nature of climate-related risks and management tools [pra_ss5-25_climate__0020__business-strategy]. Additionally, firms should conduct sensitivity analysis to understand the materiality of model choice and calibration, and consider model and input data uncertainty when interpreting Climate Scenario Analysis (CSA) results [pra_ss5-25_climate__0031__scenario-governance-controls-and-review].

The PRA expects firms to integrate climate-related risks into their Internal Capital Adequacy Assessment Process (ICAAP) and Internal Liquidity Adequacy Assessment Process (ILAAP) by developing processes to identify, quantify, and evaluate these risks within their respective planning horizons [chunk_id_2][chunk_id_0].

For the ICAAP, firms must assess the solvency impact of climate-related risks, ensuring that material risks in the firm risk register are appropriately capitalised [chunk_id_2]. This includes assessing material exposures over relevant time horizons that may negatively affect capital, incorporating physical and transition risks into stress testing programmes to evaluate financial positions under severe but plausible scenarios [chunk_id_2].

For the ILAAP, firms must identify, quantify, and evaluate climate-related risks that may materially impair liquidity and funding positions, incorporating these into internal liquidity management systems [chunk_id_0]. This involves assessing whether climate risks could cause net cash outflows or depletion of liquidity buffers under stressed scenarios, and determining if exposures subject to material climate risks are appropriately funded [chunk_id_0]. Munich Re's sustainability ambition is defined by its "Ambition 2025" programme, which is built around three guiding principles: Scale, Shape, and Succeed [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0015__ambition-2025].

Key elements of the decarbonization approach and broader sustainability strategy include:

*   **Voluntary Commitments and Frameworks:** Munich Re has joined several initiatives, including the UN Global Compact (UNGC), Principles for Responsible Investment (PRI), UNEP FI Principles for Sustainable Insurance (PSI), the Net-Zero Asset Owner Alliance (NZAOA), and the Taskforce on Nature-related Disclosures (TNFD) [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0019__meeting-voluntary-commitments].

*   **Core Values and Culture:** Diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) are core values embedded in the corporate culture, supported by a new governance structure for DEI introduced in 2023 and a Code of Conduct rolled out in 2023 that takes a clear stand against racism and discrimination [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0004__1-1-ceo-statement].

Munich Re's underwriting policy for thermal coal and oil and gas risks includes specific targets, phase-out commitments, and restrictions:

**Thermal Coal:**
*   Munich Re aims to reduce GHG emissions related to thermal coal extraction and coal-fired power plants by 35% by 2025 compared to a 2019 base year [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0153__liabilities].
*   The company has committed to completely phasing out thermal coal insurance by 2040 [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0153__liabilities].
*   Underwriting restrictions apply to new thermal coal mines, power plants, and infrastructure [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0154__3-3-climate-related-disclosure].
*   Exceptions for companies with thermal coal revenues between 15% and 30% are possible if an active engagement dialogue is established [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0077__next-steps-until-2025].

**Oil and Gas:**
*   Munich Re has set a short-term target to lower self-calculated GHG emissions associated with oil and gas production business by 5% by 2025 relative to the 2019 base year [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0153__liabilities].
*   Since 1 April 2023, Munich Re no longer insures single-risk, single-location contracts exclusively covering new oil and gas fields (where no prior production existed as of 31 December 2022), new midstream oil infrastructure (not under construction/operation as of 31 December 2022), or new oil-fired power plants (not under construction/operation as of 31 December 2022) [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0054__oil-and-gas].
*   These restrictions also apply where such risks are bundled with other risks under a single cover, provided the cover is mainly designed to protect against the new risks [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0054__oil-and-gas].
*   Munich Re does not permit facultative reinsurance or primary insurance for new or existing oil sand sites or related infrastructure [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0054__oil-and-gas].
*   Exclusions for oil sands apply to mixed policies with exposure above a threshold of 20% of the sum insured or revenue [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0054__oil-and-gas].
*   Munich Re applies underwriting restrictions and exclusions for oil and gas activities related to new fields, new midstream oil infrastructure, oil-fired power plants, oil sands, and Arctic oil and gas activities [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0154__3-3-climate-related-disclosure].

Swiss Re combines its underwriting approach with broader climate risk monitoring through a multi-step process involving quantitative and qualitative assessments, integration into financial planning, and active portfolio management.

First, Swiss Re identifies potentially relevant portfolios by applying a quantitative threshold of 5% of total premium, reserves, or expected profits, as well as selecting portfolios where weather-related perils are the dominant loss driver [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0227__underwriting]. For natural catastrophe business, annual expected losses (AEL) are also used in this identification step [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0227__underwriting].

Second, the company evaluates projected trends for climate-related risks relevant to each portfolio on a qualitative and, if possible, quantitative basis [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0227__underwriting]. These underwriting strategies are guided by Swiss Re's Group strategy, which is translated annually into a detailed financial plan bound by conditions set forth in the ESG Risk Framework [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0226__physical-and-transition-risks].

Finally, Swiss Re actively manages these portfolios by regularly adjusting proprietary probabilistic natural catastrophe models to integrate the latest scientific findings and loss experience, allowing the company to steer weather-related exposures and target risk-adequate prices through annual contract renewals [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0202__underwriting].

Swiss Re describes its single-risk (or facultative) reinsurance as a form where each risk or policy is negotiated and agreed on individually, typically used for larger commercial assets that extend beyond what is covered by a treaty reinsurance contract [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0218__single-risk-re-insurance][swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0007__swiss-re-s-business-model][swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0123__environment].

Key aspects of Swiss Re's single-risk reinsurance offering include:

*   **Climate Alignment and Targets:** Swiss Re has climate alignment targets for single-risk P&C re/insurance portfolios, aiming to gradually increase the share of Gross Written Premium (GWP) aligned to net zero by 2050. In 2024, these targets covered 23% of the total GWP of the single-risk P&C re/insurance portfolio [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0218__single-risk-re-insurance]. The company assesses clients' transition status to prioritize engagement and may use client-specific portfolio steering if engagement is ineffective [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0218__single-risk-re-insurance].
*   **Exposure Restrictions:** Swiss Re does not provide single-risk re/insurance to companies or projects with more than 30% exposure to thermal coal (since 2018) or to thermal coal assets of companies planning to develop new sites or power capacity of at least 100MW [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0138__approach-in-underwriting]. Exceptions exist for transactions in low- and middle-income countries with specific emission reduction strategies, operational carbon capture and storage, or coal mine reclamation for plants operational before 2018 [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0138__approach-in-underwriting].
*   **Environmental Guidelines:** Under the Environmental Umbrella Guideline, Swiss Re does not support single-risk transactions that severely impact biodiversity (e.g., UNESCO World Heritage Sites, IUCN protected areas, Ramsar wetlands) or involve severe, unmitigated pollution [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0123__environment].
*   **Renewable Energy Focus:** Swiss Re actively writes single-risk cover for renewable energy projects, including wind, solar, hydro, biomass, geothermal, and marine/tidal plants. In 2024, Swiss Re wrote single-risk re/insurance for 21,573 wind and solar power plants, as well as 329 other renewable power plants, representing a 45% increase from the previous year [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0138__approach-in-underwriting][swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0157__advancing-the-net-zero-transition]. This segment is viewed as having significant growth potential and contributing to the transition to a low-carbon economy [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0157__advancing-the-net-zero-transition].

The PRA expects insurers to use Climate Scenario Analysis (CSA) as a key tool to identify, quantify, and manage climate-related risks, particularly because historic data is insufficient for these risks [pra_ss5-25_climate__0027__role-of-scenario-analysis]. The specific expectations include:

*   **Proportionality and Sophistication:** The use of CSA should be proportionate to the materiality of the risks. Firms with material exposure are expected to use more sophisticated tools, potentially including a mix of narrative-based scenarios quantified by expert judgement and mathematically sophisticated approaches [pra_ss5-25_climate__0027__role-of-scenario-analysis].
*   **Integration with ORSA:** Insurers must include CSA in their Stress and Scenario Testing (SST) component of the Own Risk and Solvency Assessment (ORSA), unless the impact is immaterial [pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa]. The ORSA must explain why any material risks accepted are appropriate [pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa].
*   **Scenario Design and Granularity:** Insurers should build scenarios sufficiently granular to stress specific risks they face (e.g., tropical storms, flooding, credit risk) and use assumptions based on relevant parameters (e.g., sea surface temperatures, GDP, interest rates) [pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa]. They should consider the latest climate science and advances in modelling [pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa].
*   **Management Actions:** Insurers should specify management actions (e.g., changes to underwriting or investment strategy) and suitable trigger points for implementation in their ORSA, ensuring these actions are realistic and prudent regarding market availability and liquidity [pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa].
*   **Reputational Risk:** Insurers should consider climate-related reputational risks arising from their strategies, commitments, and potential 'greenwashing' claims within the ORSA [pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa].
*   **Governance and Review:** The board must understand the capabilities and limitations of CSA models, including uncertainties and data proxies. CSA toolkits should be subject to periodic review and challenge, considering up-to-date scientific evidence and modelling advancements [pra_ss5-25_climate__0031__scenario-governance-controls-and-review].
*   **Use Cases:** CSA should inform business strategy, risk management, capital setting, and valuation. For capital setting, it must demonstrate the link between climate risks and capital, including under stressed scenarios [pra_ss5-25_climate__0028__scenario-selection-and-use-cases__pf00].

The PRA expects insurers to include the following in their Own Risk and Solvency Assessment (ORSA) regarding climate-related risks:

*   Processes to consider the impact on capital levels of reasonably foreseeable adverse scenarios, including material climate-related risks, as part of capital management plans [pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa].
*   If a firm decides to accept a material risk, the ORSA must explain why this was considered appropriate [pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa].
*   Climate Scenario Analysis (CSA) as part of the Stress and Scenario Testing (SST) component, unless the impact is immaterial [pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa].
*   Consideration of the latest climate science and advances in climate scenario modelling [pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa].
*   Scenarios that are sufficiently granular to stress for the specific risks the insurer faces (e.g., tropical storms, flooding, longevity risk, credit risk) and build on parameters such as sea surface temperatures, precipitation, GDP, inflation, interest rates, and unemployment rates [pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa].
*   Specification of management actions to be taken in different circumstances, including the triggers for those actions (e.g., changes to underwriting or investment strategy) [pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa].
*   Consideration of climate-related reputational risks arising from investment and underwriting strategies, historical activities, and engagement on climate change and the transition to net zero [pra_ss5-25_climate__0049__own-risk-and-solvency-assessment-orsa].

According to the PRA, non-life insurers should approach underwriting risk in the context of climate change by considering the impact on natural catastrophe risk and assessing whether this impact is sufficiently factored into quantitative tools used for assessing present-day or future weather-related perils, such as tropical cyclones, flooding, droughts, and wildfires [pra_ss5-25_climate__0051__underwriting-and-reserving-risk]. Insurers should make material adjustments to reflect their own view of the impact and consider how climate change might lead to larger claims than expected from historical experience alone [pra_ss5-25_climate__0051__underwriting-and-reserving-risk]. Additionally, during model validation, insurers must explicitly consider how climate change impacts are reflected in models for climate-related perils that significantly contribute to the Solvency Capital Requirement (SCR) [pra_ss5-25_climate__0051__underwriting-and-reserving-risk].

For liability insurances, such as Directors and Officers, Product liability, and Public Liability, non-life insurers should consider the potential for an accumulation of claims due to climate change, allowing for claims to emerge under multiple contracts and underwriting years where applicable [pra_ss5-25_climate__0051__underwriting-and-reserving-risk]. Where exposures to these claims are large, the modelling of non-natural catastrophes should include an allowance for climate claims [pra_ss5-25_climate__0051__underwriting-and-reserving-risk].

Based on the provided sources, the EIOPA governance requirements for fit and proper persons are as follows:

**Fit Requirements**
National competent authorities must ensure that persons who effectively run the undertaking or hold key functions (including administrative, supervisory, or management bodies) are "fit" [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0012__guideline-11-fit-requirements]. This involves:
*   Taking into account duties allocated to individual persons to ensure appropriate diversity of qualifications, knowledge, and relevant experience [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0012__guideline-11-fit-requirements].
*   Ensuring the collective body possesses appropriate qualification, experience, and knowledge regarding:
    *   Insurance and financial markets [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0012__guideline-11-fit-requirements].
    *   Business strategy and business model [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0012__guideline-11-fit-requirements].
    *   System of governance [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0012__guideline-11-fit-requirements].
    *   Financial and actuarial analysis [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0012__guideline-11-fit-requirements].
    *   Regulatory framework and requirements [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0012__guideline-11-fit-requirements].

**Proper Requirements**
When assessing whether a person is "proper," the undertaking must assess:
*   The person's honesty and financial soundness [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0012__guideline-12-proper-requirements].
*   Relevant evidence regarding character, personal behaviour, and business conduct, including any criminal, financial, or supervisory aspects regardless of jurisdiction [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0012__guideline-12-proper-requirements].

**Policy and Procedures**
National competent authorities must ensure the undertaking has a policy on fit and proper requirements that includes:
*   A description of the procedure for assessing fitness and propriety for those running the undertaking or holding key functions, both during consideration for the position and on an ongoing basis [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0014__guideline-13-fit-and-proper-policies-and].
*   A description of situations triggering a re-assessment of fit and proper requirements [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0014__guideline-13-fit-and-proper-policies-and].
*   A description of fit and proper procedures for assessing other relevant personnel not subject to Article 42 of the Solvency II Directive, according to internal standards, both when considered for a position and on an ongoing basis [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0014__guideline-13-fit-and-proper-policies-and].

Based on the provided sources, EIOPA requires national competent authorities to ensure that undertakings adhere to the following regarding the outsourcing of key functions:

*   **Fit and Proper Procedures:** Undertakings must apply fit and proper procedures to assess persons employed by the service provider or sub-service provider performing an outsourced key function [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0015__guideline-14-outsourcing-of-key-function].
*   **Designation of Responsible Person:** The undertaking must designate a person within the undertaking with overall responsibility for the outsourced key function. This person must be fit and proper and possess sufficient knowledge and experience to challenge the performance and results of the service provider [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0015__guideline-14-outsourcing-of-key-function].
*   **Written Policy:** The undertaking’s outsourcing policy must cover its approach and processes for outsourcing from inception to the end of the contract, including:
    *   Criteria for determining if a function is critical or important [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0048__guideline-47-outsourcing-written-policy].
    *   How a suitable service provider is selected and how/often their performance is assessed [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0048__guideline-47-outsourcing-written-policy].
    *   Details to be included in the written agreement with the service provider [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0048__guideline-47-outsourcing-written-policy].
    *   Business contingency plans, including exit strategies for critical or important functions [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0048__guideline-47-outsourcing-written-policy].
*   **Determination of Criticality:** The undertaking must determine and document whether an outsourced function is critical or important based on whether it is essential to the undertaking's operation and ability to deliver services to policyholders [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0040__guideline-39-coordination-of-the-calcula].
*   **Intermediaries:** If an insurance intermediary (not an employee) is given authority to underwrite business or settle claims, this activity is subject to outsourcing requirements [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0040__guideline-39-coordination-of-the-calcula].
*   **Intra-group Outsourcing:** If key functions are outsourced within a group, the responsible entity must document which functions relate to which legal entity and ensure that the performance of key functions at the undertaking level is not impaired [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0040__guideline-39-coordination-of-the-calcula].

According to EIOPA Guidelines on System of Governance (Guideline 24), an undertaking's liquidity risk management policy should cover at least the following items [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0025__guideline-24-liquidity-risk-management-p]:

a) The procedure for determining the level of mismatch between the cash inflows and the cash outflows of both assets and liabilities, including expected cash flows of direct insurance and reinsurance such as claims, lapses or surrenders [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0025__guideline-24-liquidity-risk-management-p].
b) Consideration of total liquidity needs in the short and medium term, including an appropriate liquidity buffer to guard against a liquidity shortfall [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0025__guideline-24-liquidity-risk-management-p].
c) Consideration of the level and monitoring of liquid assets, including a quantification of potential costs or financial losses arising from an enforced realisation [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0025__guideline-24-liquidity-risk-management-p].
d) Identification and costs of alternative financing tools [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0025__guideline-24-liquidity-risk-management-p].
e) Consideration of the effect on the liquidity situation of expected new business [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0025__guideline-24-liquidity-risk-management-p].

Additionally, regarding reinsurance, the risk management policy should include liquidity management to deal with any timing mismatch between claims' payments and reinsurance recoverable [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0022__guideline-21-reinsurance-and-other-risk].

Under EIOPA's Guideline 18, an undertaking's underwriting and reserving risk management policy must cover the following items [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0019__guideline-18-underwriting-and-reserving]:

a) The types and characteristics of the insurance business, including the type of insurance risk the undertaking is willing to accept [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0019__guideline-18-underwriting-and-reserving].
b) How the adequacy of premium income to cover expected claims and expenses is to be ensured [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0019__guideline-18-underwriting-and-reserving].
c) The identification of risks arising from the undertaking's insurance obligations, including embedded options and guaranteed surrender values in its products [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0019__guideline-18-underwriting-and-reserving].
d) How, in the process of designing a new insurance product and premium calculation, the undertaking takes account of constraints related to investments [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0019__guideline-18-underwriting-and-reserving].
e) How, in the process of designing a new insurance product and premium calculation, the undertaking takes account of reinsurance or other risk mitigation techniques [eiopa_guidelines_system_of_governance__0019__guideline-18-underwriting-and-reserving].

Munich Re will not accept stand-alone covers for Arctic oil and gas drilling activities, including directly related infrastructure and installations [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0056__arctic-oil-and-gas]. This restriction also applies to mixed covers and treaty reinsurance business where the exposure exceeds a defined single-digit percentage threshold [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0056__arctic-oil-and-gas]. The policy allows minor exceptions in the Norwegian region south of the Arctic Circle [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0056__arctic-oil-and-gas]. Munich Re defines the Arctic region based on the internationally accepted definition by the Arctic Monitoring and Assessment Programme (AMAP) [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0056__arctic-oil-and-gas]. These guidelines apply to renewals of existing reinsurance treaty business with commencement dates of 2023 or later [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0056__arctic-oil-and-gas].

Munich Re stopped insuring these projects on 1 April 2023 [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0054__oil-and-gas]. A 'new' oil and gas field is defined as one where no prior production had taken place as of 31 December 2022 [munich_re_sustainability_2023__0054__oil-and-gas].

Swiss Re's Climate Transition Plan (CTP) is the company's first such plan, published as required by the Swiss Ordinance on Climate Disclosures [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0049__climate-transition-plan]. It outlines the company's transition approach and action plan for decarbonising its business while developing related opportunities [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0049__climate-transition-plan][swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0200__summary][swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0207__climate-transition-plan].

The CTP describes Swiss Re's net-zero transition approach, which is centred on a decarbonisation strategy involving three key levers: jointly transition by engaging with clients, investees and vendors; manage portfolios; and reduce operational emissions [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0049__climate-transition-plan]. It also outlines Swiss Re's carbon removal strategy and transition-enabling activities that address critical dependencies and uncertainties [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0049__climate-transition-plan]. The action plan builds on existing climate targets and mitigating actions already implemented across underwriting, investments, and operations [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0049__climate-transition-plan].

For single-risk business, Swiss Re's Thermal Coal Policy applies the following thresholds:

*   **Exposure Limit:** Swiss Re does not provide re/insurance to companies or projects with more than 30% exposure to thermal coal [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0138__approach-in-underwriting].
*   **New Projects/Capacity:** The policy restricts thermal coal assets of companies planning to develop new thermal coal mining sites or power capacity of at least 100MW [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0138__approach-in-underwriting]. This restriction applies to new clients for the property line of business as of mid-2024, and to existing clients as of mid-2025 (property) and mid-2026 (other lines of business) [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0138__approach-in-underwriting].
*   **Exceptions:** Exceptions apply for transactions in low- and middle-income countries where more than 70% of electricity derives from coal, allowing coverage of existing power plants (operational before 2018) until 2025 if an effective emission reduction strategy is implemented [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0138__approach-in-underlying]. Additional exceptions exist for transactions with operational carbon capture and storage targeting all emissions, coal mine reclamation for mines operational before 2018, and exceptional cases supporting accelerated decommissioning of thermal coal power plants [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0138__approach-in-underwriting].

Swiss Re declines to support single-risk re/insurance transactions for the following oil and gas companies:

*   Companies that produce the world's 10% most carbon-intensive oil and gas, measured as CO2 lifecycle emissions per barrel, based on Rystad data [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0135__approach-in-underwriting].
*   Companies with an annual oil and gas production of more than 10 million barrels of oil equivalent based on Rystad data [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0135__approach-in-underwriting].
*   Companies with more than 10% of upstream production located in the Arctic Monitoring and Assessment Programme (AMAP) region (excluding Norway) [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0135__approach-in-underwriting].

Additionally, Swiss Re does not support standalone upstream projects within new oil and gas fields (i.e., those that received a final investment decision after 2022) or standalone upstream oil and gas projects located in the AMAP region (excluding Norway) [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0135__approach-in-underwriting].

Swiss Re's ESG Risk Framework comprises three umbrella guidelines and six sector-specific policies [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0124__umbrella-guidelines-and-sector-specific]. The three umbrella guidelines are based on the overarching principles of protecting the environment, human and labour rights, and promoting good corporate governance [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0124__umbrella-guidelines-and-sector-specific]. The six sector-specific policies pertain to Agriculture, Forestry and Food; Defence (including Nuclear Material); Hydro Dams; Mining; Oil and Gas; and Thermal Coal [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0126__governance].

Swiss Re’s ESG risk assessment and escalation process for underwriting transactions operates as follows:

**Assessment Phase**
The process begins with an ESG risk assessment tool used to evaluate potential transactions, typically single-risk business, where information granularity allows for a meaningful assessment [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0147__introduction][swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0150__esg-risk-assessment]. This tool classifies transactions as low, medium, or high risk, or provides a direct recommendation to abstain, while offering guidance on potential ESG exposures [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0150__esg-risk-assessment]. The tool utilizes a proprietary database containing a country/sector risk matrix and a watch list of companies and projects, which is regularly updated with data from public sources and third-party providers [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0150__esg-risk-assessment]. For treaty business, assessments are conducted when data on the underlying risk is available, with specific approaches for sectors like thermal coal, defense, and oil and gas [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0150__esg-risk-assessment].

**Referral Phase**
High-risk transactions identified by the assessment tool require a mandatory ESG risk referral to internal sustainability risk managers for in-depth analysis [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0152__esg-risk-referral-and-escalation-process]. Business practitioners may also voluntarily submit referrals for any potential transaction based on additional research or underwriting judgment, even if not classified as high risk [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0152__esg-risk-referral-and-escalation-process]. Sustainability risk managers analyze these referrals using data from public sources and independent third-party research, applying a 5% materiality threshold based on the line of business, the re/insured's revenue, or the total re/insured value [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0152__esg-risk-referral-and-escalation-process].

**Outcome and Escalation**
The outcome of an ESG risk referral is a binding recommendation to proceed, proceed with conditions, or abstain [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0152__esg-risk-referral-and-escalation-process]. Conditions may require clients to provide evidence or monitor risk mitigation measures, with mandatory follow-up to verify adherence [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0152__esg-risk-referral-and-escalation-process]. If a business practitioner disagrees with an abstain or conditional recommendation, they can appeal and escalate the matter to the next management level [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0152__esg-risk-referral-and-escalation-process]. However, final decision-making authority during escalation remains with Risk Management and Swiss Re's Group Chief Risk Officer [swiss_re_sustainability_2024__0152__esg-risk-referral-and-escalation-process].

An impact tolerance is defined as the maximum tolerable level of disruption to an important business service as measured by a length of time in addition to any other relevant metrics [pra_ss1-21_operational_resilience__0004__setting-an-impact-tolerance__pf00]. It is set at the point at which any further disruption to the important business service would pose a risk to the firm's safety and soundness, and in the case of insurers, policyholder protection, and, if a firm meets the criteria as set out in the Operational Resilience Parts, the financial stability of the UK [pra_ss1-21_operational_resilience__0004__setting-an-impact-tolerance__pf00].

After 31 March 2025, the PRA expects maintaining operational resilience to be a dynamic activity [pra_ss1-21_operational_resilience__0006__setting-an-impact-tolerance__pf02]. By this date, firms should have sound, effective, and comprehensive strategies, processes, and systems that enable them to address risks to their ability to remain within their impact tolerance for each important business service in the event of a severe but plausible disruption [pra_ss1-21_operational_resilience__0006__setting-an-impact-tolerance__pf02].
